name: Parse
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service acquired by Facebook in 2013; Facebook shut the platform down in 2017, stranding thousands of dependent apps.
lesson: Building a business on top of a platform owned by a larger acquirer carries existential risk if the parent's strategy shifts.

name: StackMob
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service platform acquired by PayPal in 2013 and shut down the following year as PayPal absorbed the engineering team.
lesson: Acquihire-style acquisitions often mean the acquired product itself has no long-term future.

name: Automate.io
funding: Undisclosed seed funding, acquired by Notion 2021
summary: iPaaS workflow automation tool acquired by Notion in 2021 and fully discontinued in 2023 as Notion built native automation instead.
lesson: An acquirer may value the team and IP more than the product itself, leading to eventual sunset.

name: Kissmetrics
summary: Customer analytics platform that struggled to differentiate against Mixpanel and Amplitude and shut down its product in 2018.
lesson: Analytics tools need a durable technical or distribution edge to survive well-funded direct competitors.

name: RJMetrics
summary: Early e-commerce analytics and data pipeline company acquired by Magento in 2016; Adobe discontinued the standalone product after the Magento acquisition.
lesson: Being acquired for a parent's product roadmap can mean the original product is deprioritized and killed.

name: Keen IO
summary: Event analytics API startup that shut down its service in 2021 after failing to reach sustainable scale against larger analytics platforms.
lesson: Developer-analytics infrastructure is a crowded market where undifferentiated APIs struggle to retain customers long-term.

name: DataHero
summary: Self-service BI and data visualization startup that shut down in 2016 after failing to gain enough traction against bigger BI incumbents.
lesson: Self-serve BI tools need a wedge beyond charts and dashboards to avoid being outgunned by Tableau-class incumbents.

name: BIME Analytics
summary: French cloud BI platform acquired by Zendesk in 2015 to build Zendesk Explore; the standalone BIME product was discontinued in 2018.
lesson: An acquisition aimed at absorbing technology into a new product line often ends the acquired brand's independent life.
source: Zendesk BIME acquisition and Explore transition announcements

name: Roambi
summary: Mobile BI visualization app acquired by Actuate (later OpenText) that was formally discontinued in 2020 as OpenText consolidated its analytics portfolio.
lesson: Point-solution BI tools risk being folded into and eventually retired from a larger vendor's product suite.

name: Platfora
summary: Big-data visualization startup that raised over $100M but was acquired by Workday for a fraction of that in 2016; Workday discontinued the product in 2017.
lesson: Heavy capital consumption without proportional revenue growth in enterprise BI often ends in a distressed sale rather than an exit.

name: MapR
summary: Hadoop distribution and big-data platform that raised huge sums but ran out of cash in 2019 and sold its assets to HPE in a distressed fire sale.
lesson: Infrastructure companies built around a specific big-data stack can be stranded when the broader ecosystem (Hadoop) declines faster than expected.

name: Cloudera
summary: Hadoop-based data platform that IPO'd in 2017, merged with rival Hortonworks in 2019, and was taken private by KKR/CD&R in 2021 at a valuation far below its IPO-era peak.
lesson: A public listing does not guarantee sustained success if the core technology bet loses relevance to newer cloud-native alternatives.
source: Cloudera S-1, Hortonworks merger and 2021 take-private filings

name: Cazena
summary: Managed data-lake-as-a-service startup that shut down in 2021 as cloud providers' native data lake offerings made its managed layer redundant.
lesson: A managed-service wrapper around cloud infrastructure can be commoditized once hyperscalers build the same capability natively.

name: Grouparoo
summary: Open-source reverse ETL startup that wound down operations in 2023, with its team and technology joining Airbyte.
lesson: Being early to a hot category (reverse ETL) is not sufficient if better-funded competitors capture the market faster.

name: Meroxa
summary: Real-time data integration and change-data-capture platform that shut down in 2024 after failing to reach sustainable growth.
lesson: Real-time data infrastructure requires deep enterprise trust and long sales cycles that can outlast available runway.

name: Bizness Apps
summary: DIY mobile app builder for small businesses that shut down operations in 2019 as the market for generic white-label app builders shrank.
lesson: White-label no-code app builders targeting SMBs face brutal price competition and shrinking demand as native mobile web improves.

name: AppMakr
summary: One of the earliest DIY mobile app builders, it quietly shut down around 2018 after losing ground to more modern no-code platforms.
lesson: First-mover advantage in no-code tooling erodes quickly if the platform fails to modernize its underlying technology.
source: Industry retrospectives on early app-builder platforms

name: Como
summary: Israeli DIY app-builder platform for small businesses that shut down its service in 2018 despite substantial venture funding.
lesson: Consumer-facing SMB app builders need a low-cost, high-retention model since churn among small-business customers is inherently high.
source: Como shutdown coverage, Israeli tech press 2018

name: Shoutem
summary: Croatian no-code mobile app builder that ceased operations in 2021 after a decade of competing in the crowded app-builder space.
lesson: Small, thinly-capitalized players in a commoditized no-code category struggle to outlast well-funded competitors.

name: AppMachine
summary: Dutch no-code app builder acquired by GoDaddy in 2015 to power its website/app bundle; GoDaddy discontinued the product in 2018.
lesson: A strategic acquisition to bundle into an acquirer's product line often has a limited shelf life once strategy shifts again.
source: GoDaddy AppMachine acquisition and later discontinuation coverage

name: Podio
summary: Danish no-code work-management platform acquired by Citrix in 2012; Citrix formally sunset Podio in 2023 after years of limited investment.
lesson: Products acquired as adjacent bets inside a larger portfolio can be starved of investment and eventually retired.

name: WorkFusion
summary: RPA and intelligent-automation platform that repeatedly pivoted (general RPA to AML/compliance-focused automation) after failing to compete with UiPath and Automation Anywhere at scale.
lesson: Repeated strategic pivots signal an unresolved product-market fit problem that erodes investor and customer confidence over time.
source: Industry coverage of WorkFusion's compliance pivot

name: Zoomdata
summary: Streaming-data visualization startup acquired by Micro Focus's Vertica unit in 2019; the product line was discontinued roughly two years later.
lesson: BI point products acquired into a database vendor's stack often get deprioritized versus the vendor's core offering.
source: Micro Focus Vertica/Zoomdata acquisition and later sunset

name: Metanautix
summary: Big-data query and integration startup acquired by Microsoft in 2016; its technology was folded into Microsoft's data platform and not sold as a standalone product.
lesson: An acqui-hire can be a positive outcome for founders while still ending the product as an independent entity.

name: AnyPresence
summary: Enterprise mobile backend and app-platform startup that ceased independent operations in 2016 after failing to scale against larger MBaaS competitors.
lesson: Enterprise mobile backend platforms needed either massive scale or a defensible niche to survive the MBaaS shakeout of the mid-2010s.
source: Industry reporting on mobile backend-as-a-service consolidation

name: Antenna Software
summary: Early enterprise mobile app platform that failed to transition its licensing model to the cloud era and was absorbed into Verivo Software.
lesson: Legacy enterprise software vendors that fail to re-architect for cloud delivery become acquisition targets rather than survivors.

name: Verivo Software
summary: Enterprise mobile app platform (successor to Antenna Software) that shut down operations in 2015 amid the broader enterprise-mobility platform shakeout.
lesson: Consolidating with a struggling competitor rarely fixes an underlying product-market fit problem.
source: Industry reporting on Verivo Software shutdown 2015

name: 8base
summary: GraphQL backend-as-a-service and low-code platform that shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Backend-as-a-service platforms compete directly with well-funded hyperscaler offerings (Firebase, AWS Amplify), which raises the bar for differentiation.

name: CloudBoost
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service startup (an early Parse alternative) that shut down its service in 2018 after struggling to build a sustainable customer base.
lesson: Building a replacement for a shuttered platform (Parse) does not guarantee demand if the underlying market has already moved on.

name: GoodData
summary: Embedded analytics and BI platform that underwent major layoffs and a distressed sale/restructuring around 2023 after years of struggling to match cloud-native BI competitors.
lesson: Being an early mover in cloud BI is not enough if the product fails to keep pace with newer, more self-service analytics platforms.

name: Sisense
summary: Embedded BI and analytics platform once valued over $1B that suffered a major security breach in 2024 and was subsequently sold at a distressed valuation.
lesson: A major security incident can rapidly destroy enterprise trust and valuation even for an established analytics vendor.
source: Sisense breach disclosure and acquisition coverage 2024

name: IFTTT
summary: Consumer workflow-automation pioneer ('If This Then That') that underwent severe layoffs and near-collapse of its team in 2023 after failing to convert its huge user base into a sustainable business.
lesson: A large free user base does not guarantee a viable business model without a clear path to monetization.
source: Reporting on IFTTT layoffs and business struggles 2023

name: Birst
summary: Cloud BI platform acquired by Infor in 2017 for roughly $150M; the Birst brand was ultimately discontinued as Infor consolidated its analytics stack around 2022.
lesson: Raising very large sums for a BI platform doesn't guarantee an independent future if the eventual acquirer folds the brand into its own roadmap.
source: Infor Birst acquisition and later product consolidation

name: Appcelerator
summary: Cross-platform mobile app development platform acquired by Axway in 2016; the product was declared end-of-life around 2020 as Axway shifted strategy.
lesson: Mobile-first no-code/low-code platforms from the early smartphone era struggled to keep up once native and web-based alternatives matured.

name: Alooma
summary: Israeli data-pipeline/ETL startup acquired by Google Cloud in 2019; Google discontinued the standalone product around 2022 in favor of its own Data Fusion offering.
lesson: Being acquired by a cloud hyperscaler often means the acquired product gets absorbed and eventually replaced by the parent's native tooling.
source: Google Cloud Alooma acquisition and later sunset notices

name: Backand
summary: Low-code backend-as-a-service platform that ceased active operations around 2018 after failing to scale against larger BaaS competitors.
lesson: Thinly-capitalized backend-as-a-service startups have little room for error in a market dominated by well-funded incumbents.

name: Kinvey
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service platform acquired by Progress Software in 2017; Progress discontinued Kinvey in 2022 as part of a product portfolio rationalization.
lesson: Even a successful acquisition exit can end in product discontinuation years later if the category consolidates around the acquirer's core business.

name: Built.io
summary: API and backend integration platform acquired by Software AG in 2018; the standalone product line was discontinued shortly after as it was merged into Software AG's integration suite.
lesson: Integration-layer startups acquired for their technology are frequently merged and rebranded out of existence.
source: Software AG Built.io acquisition and integration coverage

name: Chartio
summary: Self-service BI and dashboarding tool acquired by Atlassian in late 2021; Atlassian shut it down almost immediately in 2022, giving customers only months to migrate.
lesson: An acquihire can result in near-immediate shutdown of the acquired product, leaving customers with very short migration windows.

name: Hortonworks
summary: Hadoop distribution company that IPO'd in 2014 but never reached sustained profitability, eventually merging with rival Cloudera in 2019 in an all-stock 'merger of equals.'
lesson: Open-source-based infrastructure businesses can struggle to monetize enough to justify heavy venture and public-market capital raised.

name: Domo
summary: Business intelligence and dashboarding platform that IPO'd in 2018 but has seen its stock price fall over 90% since, with persistent unprofitability despite the massive pre-IPO capital raised.
lesson: A successful IPO does not offset years of extreme cash burn if the underlying unit economics never turn sustainably profitable.
source: Domo SEC filings and public market performance

name: Prisma
summary: Berlin-based startup that shut down its hosted Prisma Cloud data-layer product in 2022 after a strategic pivot, laying off staff to refocus entirely on its open-source ORM.
lesson: When a startup's original hosted-platform business model fails to gain traction, refocusing on a narrower open-source niche can be the only path to survival.

name: Paxata
summary: Self-service data preparation platform acquired by DataRobot in 2019; the standalone product was discontinued shortly after as DataRobot integrated its technology.
lesson: Data-prep tools raised huge sums competing for a market that hyperscalers and BI vendors later absorbed natively.

name: Sencha
summary: JavaScript/Ext JS app-development framework company sold to Idera in 2019 in what was widely viewed as a distressed sale after years of declining growth.
lesson: Framework and tooling vendors that fail to keep pace with faster-moving open-source ecosystems eventually lose commercial relevance.

name: Bubble
summary: No-code web app builder that grew into one of the largest independent no-code platforms, powering hundreds of thousands of applications.
lesson: A general-purpose no-code platform can achieve durable scale by staying laser-focused on developer/builder ergonomics over many years.

name: Webflow
summary: Visual, no-code website design and CMS platform that scaled to a multi-billion-dollar valuation serving designers and marketing teams.
lesson: Targeting a specific professional persona (designers) with a best-in-class visual tool can command premium valuations in no-code.

name: Airtable
summary: Spreadsheet-database hybrid no-code platform that became a default tool for flexible business workflows across a huge range of industries.
lesson: A flexible, horizontal data tool can expand far beyond its original use case when it captures a broad developer and non-developer audience alike.

name: Zapier
summary: Workflow automation platform connecting thousands of apps that grew to profitability with minimal venture funding, becoming a category-defining iPaaS tool.
lesson: A capital-efficient, profitable growth path is achievable in SaaS infrastructure when the core value proposition (app integrations) has broad, immediate utility.
source: Zapier company history and founder interviews

name: Notion
summary: All-in-one workspace and no-code database/wiki tool that scaled to tens of millions of users and a multi-billion-dollar valuation.
lesson: Combining flexible no-code data structures with strong design and virality can drive rapid bottoms-up enterprise adoption.

name: Retool
summary: Low-code platform for building internal business tools that became a standard choice for engineering teams needing fast internal app development.
lesson: Focusing narrowly on internal tools (a less glamorous but high-value niche) let Retool avoid head-on competition with consumer-facing no-code platforms.

name: Appian
summary: Enterprise low-code process automation platform that went public in 2017 and remains a major public enterprise software company.
lesson: Long-term focus on enterprise process automation can sustain a company for decades and through a successful public offering.
source: Appian IPO prospectus and public filings

name: OutSystems
summary: Portuguese enterprise low-code development platform that reached a multi-billion-dollar valuation by focusing on large-scale enterprise application development.
lesson: European enterprise software companies can achieve outsized valuations by owning a defensible enterprise low-code niche globally.

name: Mendix
summary: Dutch low-code application platform acquired by Siemens in 2018 for $730M to power its industrial IoT and digital-twin strategy.
lesson: A low-code platform with strong enterprise adoption can command a premium acquisition price from an industrial conglomerate seeking digital transformation capability.

name: Wix
summary: Israeli no-code website builder that went public in 2013 and grew into a multi-billion-dollar company serving over 200 million users.
lesson: Consumer no-code website builders can achieve massive scale through freemium acquisition and continuous product expansion.
source: Wix IPO prospectus, public financial filings

name: Squarespace
funding: IPO 2021, taken private by Permira 2024 for $6.9B
summary: No-code website and e-commerce builder that IPO'd in 2021 and was later taken private by Permira in 2024 in a deal valuing it at $6.9B.
lesson: A profitable, design-focused no-code product can sustain strong valuations across both public and private ownership phases.
source: Squarespace IPO filing and Permira acquisition announcement 2024

name: monday.com
summary: Israeli no-code work-management platform that went public in 2021 and has grown into a major player in workflow and project management software.
lesson: A flexible, visual no-code work-OS can expand from project management into a broad platform play through continuous feature layering.

name: Smartsheet
funding: IPO 2018, taken private by Blackstone/Vista 2025 for $8.4B
summary: Spreadsheet-style work-management and automation platform that IPO'd in 2018 and was taken private in a $8.4B deal in 2025.
lesson: Enterprise no-code work-management platforms can sustain long-term value creation across multiple ownership structures.

name: Asana
summary: Work-management and workflow platform co-founded by a former Facebook engineer that went public via direct listing in 2020.
lesson: A workflow tool with strong product-led growth can reach public-market scale even amid intense competition in the category.

name: ClickUp
summary: All-in-one productivity and project-management platform that scaled rapidly through aggressive feature expansion and marketing to reach a multi-billion valuation.
lesson: Rapid, broad feature expansion can be an effective wedge strategy to displace multiple point-solution incumbents at once.

name: Trello
summary: Kanban-style visual project management tool spun out of Fog Creek Software that was acquired by Atlassian in 2017 for $425M.
lesson: A simple, highly visual collaboration tool can achieve viral bottoms-up growth and a strong strategic acquisition outcome.

name: AppSheet
funding: Acquired by Google 2020 for a reported $200M+
summary: No-code mobile and web app builder built on top of spreadsheets and databases, acquired by Google in 2020 to anchor its Google Workspace no-code strategy.
lesson: Building a no-code tool tightly integrated with a widely used data source (spreadsheets) can make a company a natural acquisition target for a platform owner.

name: Firebase
summary: Real-time backend-as-a-service platform for mobile and web apps, acquired by Google in 2014 and grown into a cornerstone of Google's developer platform.
lesson: A backend infrastructure product with strong developer love can become a durable, growing platform under the right acquirer.

name: Fivetran
summary: Automated data-pipeline (ELT) company that became a category leader connecting hundreds of data sources to cloud data warehouses.
lesson: Focusing relentlessly on reliability and breadth of pre-built connectors can create a durable moat in data-integration infrastructure.

name: Airbyte
summary: Open-source data-integration platform that rapidly built a large connector ecosystem and a commercial cloud offering on top of community adoption.
lesson: An open-source-first strategy can accelerate connector-ecosystem growth faster than a closed-source competitor could achieve alone.

name: Segment
summary: Customer data platform that unified event tracking and data routing across marketing and analytics tools, acquired by Twilio in 2020 for $3.2B.
lesson: Owning the customer data layer between many downstream tools creates powerful lock-in and strategic acquisition value.

name: Databricks
summary: Unified data and AI platform built around Apache Spark that grew into one of the most valuable private data-infrastructure companies in the world.
lesson: Commercializing a widely adopted open-source project (Spark) with a strong managed cloud offering can build enormous enterprise value.

name: Snowflake
funding: IPO 2020 (largest software IPO at the time)
summary: Cloud data warehouse platform that had one of the largest software IPOs in history in 2020, becoming a dominant force in cloud data infrastructure.
lesson: Decoupling storage and compute in a cloud-native data warehouse architecture created a durable technical advantage over legacy incumbents.

name: Confluent
summary: Streaming-data platform built around Apache Kafka founded by its original creators, which went public in 2021.
lesson: Founders commercializing their own widely adopted open-source project can build a large, durable public company around it.

name: Looker
summary: Modern BI and data-modeling platform acquired by Google Cloud in 2020 for $2.6B to anchor its analytics strategy.
lesson: A modeling-layer approach to BI (LookML) created enough differentiation to command a multi-billion-dollar strategic acquisition.

name: Tableau
summary: Pioneering data-visualization and BI company that IPO'd in 2013 and was acquired by Salesforce in 2019 in one of the largest software acquisitions of that year.
lesson: Category-defining product quality in visualization/BI can sustain value creation from IPO through a mega-acquisition years later.

name: MuleSoft
summary: API and enterprise integration platform (Anypoint Platform) that IPO'd in 2017 and was acquired by Salesforce in 2018 for $6.5B.
lesson: Enterprise integration infrastructure is a high-value category that large software vendors will pay a premium to acquire.

name: Qlik
funding: IPO 2010, taken private by Thoma Bravo 2016 for $3B
summary: Swedish BI and data analytics pioneer that IPO'd in 2010 and was taken private by Thoma Bravo in 2016 for roughly $3B, continuing to operate as a major analytics vendor.
lesson: Long-running BI vendors can transition successfully from public markets to private-equity ownership while continuing to grow.

name: Talend
funding: IPO 2016, taken private by Thoma Bravo 2021 for $2.4B
summary: French open-source-rooted data integration and ETL company that IPO'd on Nasdaq in 2016 and was taken private by Thoma Bravo in 2021 for $2.4B.
lesson: Open-source-based data integration vendors can achieve strong outcomes by combining community adoption with a robust enterprise commercial layer.

name: dbt Labs
summary: Creator of the dbt (data build tool) analytics engineering framework, originally Fishtown Analytics, that became the standard for SQL-based data transformation.
lesson: Defining a new engineering discipline (analytics engineering) around an open-source tool can create category-defining commercial value.
source: dbt Labs Series D funding announcement 2022

name: Workato
summary: Enterprise integration and workflow automation platform (iPaaS) that scaled to a multi-billion valuation serving large enterprise customers.
lesson: Targeting enterprise-grade integration and automation needs, rather than just SMB workflows, can support much higher valuations.

name: Make (Integromat)
summary: Czech visual workflow-automation platform (rebranded from Integromat to Make) acquired by process-mining company Celonis in 2024 to expand its automation capabilities.
lesson: A strong visual automation product can become a strategic acquisition target for adjacent enterprise software categories like process mining.

name: Matillion
summary: UK-founded cloud ETL/ELT data-transformation platform tightly integrated with Snowflake, Redshift, and BigQuery that reached unicorn status.
lesson: Deep native integration with leading cloud data warehouses can be a durable growth strategy for an ETL vendor.

name: Dataiku
summary: French enterprise data-science and MLOps platform combining low-code and code-based workflows that scaled to unicorn status serving large enterprises.
lesson: Bridging low-code accessibility for business analysts with full coding flexibility for data scientists can widen a platform's addressable market.

name: Blue Prism
summary: UK robotic process automation (RPA) pioneer that IPO'd on the London Stock Exchange in 2016 and was acquired by SS&C Technologies in 2022 for $1.6B.
lesson: Being an early pioneer in a new automation category (RPA) can lead to a strong long-term acquisition outcome even amid heavy competition.

name: UiPath
summary: Romanian-founded robotic process automation leader that scaled globally and went public on the NYSE in 2021 at a multi-billion-dollar valuation.
lesson: A strong enterprise automation product combined with aggressive global go-to-market can carry a company from a niche RPA vendor to a public-market leader.
